posts.caption:
Stories, like the deities and demigods that define the mythology of ancient Greece, are immortal. But it’s a curious fact that, while the Roman sword-and-sandal flick has long been a part of Hollywood’s repertoire, Greek epics are altogether harder to come by. ⁠
⁠
Director Christopher Nolan has cited this gap as a motivating force in his decision to bring Homer’s epic poem The Odyssey to the screen. But Homer’s telling of the story is just a surviving relic from an oral tradition spanning a thousand years of ancient Greek culture. Just as each narrator would have brought their own slant to the material with their own particular audience in mind, filmmakers have been borrowing story beats and archetypes from Greek mythology since the dawn of cinema – they just don’t always advertise it that way.⁠
⁠
Before you take to the seas with Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey, @hannah_benson15 lists the Greek myth-inspired films to start you on your journey. Read the list at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 (Film stills)
posts.alt_text:
In less than two decades, @anamendietaartist made a profound impact on contemporary art. 

Born in Cuba in 1948, she was exiled to the US at the age of 12. She first trained as a painter, then embraced a gutsy, experimental range of art forms, at times using animal blood from butchers, plants, burnt wood and her own body in the work. 

In 1985, at the age of 36, Mendieta fell from her New York apartment window to her death. Her husband, artist Carl Andre, was charged with pushing her, and later acquitted; those who knew her and many in the art world still strongly believe he murdered her. This violent death has at times threatened to overshadow her formidable oeuvre, reducing her to the victim of a horrific crime. 

As @tate Modern opens the first in-depth UK exhibition of Mendieta’s work for more than a decade, @emilycsteer explores the defining aspects of her practice. Read the guide at the link in bio 📲

📸 1. Ana Mendieta, Imágen de Yágul 1973© The Estate of Ana Mendieta Collection, LLC. Licensed by Artist Rights Society (ARS), New York / DACS, 2026 / Courtesy Marian Goodman Gallery and Alison Jacques, London
2. Ana Mendieta, Bird Run 1974 © The Estate of Ana Mendieta Collection, LLC. Licensed by Artist Rights Society (ARS), New York / DACS, 2026 / Courtesy Marian Goodman Gallery and Alison Jacques, London
3. Ana Mendieta, Untitled c.1984 © The Estate of Ana Mendieta Collection, LLC. Licensed by Artist Rights Society (ARS), New York / DACS, 2026 / Courtesy Marian Goodman Gallery and Alison Jacques, London
4. Ana Mendieta, Untitled: Silueta Series 1976 © The Estate of Ana Mendieta Collection, LLC. Licensed by Artist Rights Society (ARS), New York / DACS, 2026 / Courtesy Marian Goodman Gallery and Alison Jacques, London
5. Ana Mendieta, Untitled (Guanaroca [First Woman]) 1981 © The Estate of Ana Mendieta Collection, LLC. Licensed by Artist Rights Society (ARS), New York / DACS, 2026 / Courtesy Marian Goodman Gallery and Alison Jacques, London
6. Ana Mendieta, Untitled 1972 © The Estate of Ana Mendieta Collection, LLC. Licensed by Artist Rights Society (ARS), New York / DACS, 2026 / Courtesy Marian Goodman Gallery and Alison Jacques, London

Stories, like the deities and demigods that define the mythology of ancient Greece, are immortal. But it’s a curious fact that, while the Roman sword-and-sandal flick has long been a part of Hollywood’s repertoire, Greek epics are altogether harder to come by. ⁠
⁠
Director Christopher Nolan has cited this gap as a motivating force in his decision to bring Homer’s epic poem The Odyssey to the screen. But Homer’s telling of the story is just a surviving relic from an oral tradition spanning a thousand years of ancient Greek culture. Just as each narrator would have brought their own slant to the material with their own particular audience in mind, filmmakers have been borrowing story beats and archetypes from Greek mythology since the dawn of cinema – they just don’t always advertise it that way.⁠
⁠
Before you take to the seas with Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey, @hannah_benson15 lists the Greek myth-inspired films to start you on your journey. Read the list at the link in bio 📲⁠

posts.caption:
For its new Autumn 2026 campaign, Escape to the Countryside, @burberry's idea of Britishness becomes a road trip through the rolling British hills, ending at Deene Park in Northamptonshire. ⁠
⁠
Among the friends folded into Burberry’s dream of town-to-country freedom is @mosesmartin, making his first ever fashion campaign appearance alongside Edie Campbell, Nora Attal and Sang Woo Kim. Martin, the son of Gwyneth Paltrow and Chris Martin, comes with a famous surname, but with music in his blood he’s not standing in its shadow. His band, @peopleivemet, soundtracks the adventure. ⁠
⁠
As he stars in the latest Burberry campaign, Martin answers questions about his band, the outdoors and his favourite British food. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #Burberry Autumn 2026 Campaign. Courtesy of Burberry⁠

posts.caption:
From Wexford in Ireland, Central Saint Martins graduate @richardmalone imbues their colourful pieces with traditional craftsmanship, using skills passed down through their maternal lineage. ⁠
⁠
In 2015, Malone debuted at London Fashion Week with Fashion East, and more recently, their fashion practice has stretched into sculpture – Malone’s billowing indigo piece took centre stage at The Royal Academy’s 2023 Summer Exhibition. Now, they are heading up an ambitious project for Ireland’s Presidency of the Council of the European Union 2026. ⁠
⁠
Cuimhne agus Séadchomhartha (Memory and Monument) will show across several official spaces in Brussels, spotlighting a host of Irish artists while uncovering shared material histories throughout Europe. “I’m really interested in this recent Irish renaissance, which is also quite farcical,” they tell @emilycsteer, when we speak ahead of the opening. “It’s really focused on the industries that produce lots of cash: major movie stars, people winning Oscars … ”⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Lead image: Richard Malone. Photography by @donal.talbot⁠
2-7. Cuimhne agus Séadchomhartha installation imagery © @hugardandvanoverschelde⁠
⁠
#RichardMalone⁠

posts.caption:
@sofiacoppola has long understood the rich texture of adolescence. @importantflowers, the publishing imprint she established with @mack_publishing, extends that eye across film, photography, art and fashion, and its latest title feels at home. ⁠
⁠
Here, AnOther can exclusively preview American Teen, a 240-page book bringing together @josephszabophotos’s renowned and previously unseen photographs of his students on Long Island during the 1970s and 1980s, with new texts by commissioning editor Coppola and Sonic Youth’s @kimletgordon.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, find out more about Joseph Szabo's work 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Joseph Szabo, from American Teen (Important Flowers, 2026). Courtesy of the artist and @mack_publishing

posts.caption:
When she was 19, @charlottegainsbourg “wasn’t well. I was a mess”. Her famous father, Serge Gainsbourg – France’s beloved chanteur-provocateur – invited her to move back in with him and recuperate at his home, 5 bis rue de Verneuil in Paris. “Everything was ready for me to step back into my childhood house with him and try to pull the pieces of my life together,” she recalls. “Then he died so suddenly.”⁠
⁠
For some 30 years, Charlotte kept her father’s house preserved as he’d left it – a “private mausoleum” where she could visit and grieve, away from the cemetery and the curious gaze and unwanted attentions of the general public. Then, in 2023, the house became a museum. But before Maison Gainsbourg opened its doors as a heritage site, Charlotte used her Hasselblad camera to document the place. ⁠
⁠
Now, these evocative pictures are on display in her debut photography exhibition, 5 Bis, curated with the help of @ysl creative director @anthonyvaccarello. The show, running as part of this year’s world-renowned @rencontresarles festival, is held in The Cloister Gallery – a sequestered space entered via a doorway on one of the medieval town’s narrow, picturesque streets. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @ohnoitsemilyd speaks with Gainsbourg about immortalising her father’s Parisian home as she remembered it from her youth 📲 ⁠
⁠
📸 5 Bis by #CharlotteGainsbourg⁠
#SergeGainsbourg

posts.caption:
PERI ROSENZWEIG — THE ARCHITECT OF POP’S NEW DARK GLAMOUR
If fashion styling is the art of building identities, Peri Rosenzweig is one of its most compelling world-builders. The New York-based stylist and creative director has quietly become one of the defining visual forces behind contemporary music and fashion, creating the hyper-stylized, futuristic aesthetics shaping a new generation of artists.
From the gothic futurism of Lady Gaga’s recent era to the glam-industrial universe of Yves Tumor, Rosenzweig’s work exists at the intersection of couture, underground club culture, punk, fetishwear and science fiction.Her collaborations span some of the most influential names in music and fashion today — including Lady Gaga, Yves Tumor, Travis Scott, Julia Fox, Grimes, King Princess, Eartheater, 070 Shake and Charli XCX.
Together with creative partner Nick Royal, she co-founded HARDSTYLE, the image-making studio responsible for some of the most distinctive visual identities in contemporary pop culture, blurring the boundaries between styling, costume design and creative direction.
Rather than dressing celebrities, Rosenzweig creates worlds around them. Her signature aesthetic is unmistakable: dark glamour, post-internet surrealism, subcultural references and a fearless approach to silhouette and texture that feels equally at home in underground nightlife and haute couture.
In an era where image is inseparable from music, Peri Rosenzweig is helping define what the future looks like.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @perirosenzweig , @terrencefoconnor
#PeriRosenzweig #FashionStylist #CreativeDirection #HARDSTYLE #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
@chanel.beauty launched its Summer’s Calling Collection, featuring the exclusive DUO DE BAUMES. This multi-use blush and highlighter duo stick enhances and illuminates both the complexion and lips.
Available in three shades: PURE GLOW, GENTLE GLOW, and INTENSE GLOW. Designed for effortless application, each shade can be worn individually, mixed and matched, or layered to create your desired look. After trying it firsthand, our editor found that the colour appears even more sheer and luminous on the skin than it does in the stick.
Combining two functions in one effortless stick, it’s perfect for everyday summer looks and holiday getaways.
Swipe right to discover all the details.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @a2z_fashionmag

posts.caption:
Shakira @shakira closed out the halftime show and stole the whole thing. 💃🔥
She came out in custom Roberto Cavalli @roberto_cavalli , designed by the house's creative director Fausto Puglisi , and hand-embroidered with around 200,000 Swarovski crystals in hot pink, orange, and citrine — yes, 200,000, and it reportedly took about 120 hours to put together. The design had deep cutouts running down both sides of the torso, a plunging neckline laced up with cords, and a high-low fringe skirt that trailed behind her every time she moved.
There's also a nice little callback here — she wore Cavalli at the World Cup back in 2010 too, so this brings her World Cup style full circle after 16 years, though this version leaned a lot more theatrical than the bohemian festival vibe she had back then.
She closed the show performing "Dai Dai" alongside Burna Boy @burnaboygram and Uganda's Ghetto Kids @ghettokids_tfug , and somehow did the entire thing barefoot while still commanding the whole field.
What was your favorite look from the halftime show? 👇
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @shakira , @roberto_cavalli , @fifaworldcup
#Shakira #RobertoCavalli #WorldCupFinal #Swarovski #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.alt_text:
Gentle Monster just dropped VEGGIE, a collection of “folding eyewear” inspired by stems and organic shapes found in nature.

The collection combines natural vegetable-like shapes with a sleek, industrial design across ten compact foldable frames with subtle, playful temple details.

Each piece comes with a leather case that doubles as a bag charm or a key chain accessory.

posts.caption:
From #TheGreatGatsby’s Jordan Baker to Diana, Princess of Wales, #ElizabethDebicki has inhabited some of popular culture’s most recognisable figures.
In our July cover story, the award-winning actor reflects on finding the humanity behind iconic characters and why every role begins with a blank slate. “But personally, I’m interested in trying to shed that and create as much open space to imagine, as you would with a character that’s entirely your own creation.”
Discover the full interview at the link in our bio.
The July issue of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia is available now. Discover the full story via the link in our bio.

“My vetting process when I read a script is asking myself, if someone called me up and said you have to go on set next week, how excited am I about doing that?”

posts.caption:
Strength isn’t always loud. Sometimes, it’s quiet curiosity, unwavering kindness, and the courage to keep moving forward. Harper’s Bazaar India’s June-July cover star, Saffron Vadher (@saffron), reflects on resilience, identity, fashion, and the people who shape us in a conversation that’s as moving as it is inspiring. “I’ve always believed that life’s best moments are the ones you share. It’s never really been about where I am or what I’m doing. It’s about who I am doing it with,” she says.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Jhanvi Duggal (@jhanvii.23)
Photographer: Vivek Vadoliya (@vivekvad); Agency, East Co (@east__co)

Saffron is wearing Haryma necklace in yellow gold, topazes, onyx, garnets, emeralds, and diamonds by Cartier (@cartier); Outfit, yellow embroidered long dress, Iris leather gloves, Balenciaga (@balenciaga), paired with satin and rhinestone pumps by Manolo Blahnik x Balenciagia collection (@manoloblahnik)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #TheNowIssue

Sonali Bendre (@iamsonalibendre), in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved), reflects on the evolving portrayal of women in cinema, why authenticity has become one of the rarest qualities today, and what it really means to be an ‘it girl’. She also speaks about the authors who shaped her worldview and how social media has changed the conversation around beauty, not just for women, but for men too.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full conversation.

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)

posts.caption:
While Spain was undeniably the biggest winner of this year’s FIFA World Cup, fashion was not far behind.
The five-week-long tournament was the industry’s biggest sports marketing spectacle since the LVMH-sponsored Paris Olympics in 2024. There were endless opportunities for brand-building moments, and not just for the sportswear labels seen on the pitch like @Adidas and @Nike.
Data from the 2026 tournament reveals how a mix of reactive tactics, organic player influence and high-heat collaborations drove the real commercial value beyond official sponsorships. Read more #linkinbio
✍️ @leitakanashi
posts.alt_text:
The beauty industry runs on celebrities (as much as on product), whose endorsements spread awareness and support marketing activations. 

But as the trade has shifted towards influencer recommendations, the value of traditional celebrity partnerships is waning, prompting beauty brands to hone in on underexposed or elusive new faces hoping they will have an impact. The challenge is getting to them first. 

BoF's Liz Flora (@liz_flora) examines how beauty brands are rethinking celebrity partnerships. Read more #linkinbio

📷 @rarebeauty, @rhode, Getty Images, @maccosmetics, @skims

posts.alt_text:
Diversity and body positivity have been important themes shaping the fashion conversation over the past decade. Yet a gap remains between the industry’s ideals and its reality. Few people have experienced that gap – and pushed back against it – more visibly than Paloma Elsesser (@palomija). She’s walked the biggest runways, landed the biggest covers and used her platform to challenge the industry’s standards around bodies, beauty and who gets to be seen.

Now, she’s taking that authority somewhere new: building a body care brand rooted in the rituals she grew up with.

“My mother’s African American. You don’t get out of the shower and not put lotion on. That’s crazy,” says Elsesser. “The central tenets of my work obviously are deeply rooted within the body, but I would love to see body care be personal and esoteric and ritualistic and beautiful,” she continues. “I think what’s so powerful about this expansive tapestry of beauty that we have today is that so many different types of people get to meet themselves and find belonging in those different brands.”

Elsesser sat down with executive editor @PriyaRao on stage at this year’s Business of Beauty Global Forum to discuss staying true to herself amid the pressures of tokenism and why — after a decade spent building other people’s brands — she’s finally thinking about building one of her own.

🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #BoFPodcast
📷 Getty Images for The Business of Fashion

#BoFBeauty

posts.caption:
Earlier this month, Gap rereleased its “Happy Stripe” design, reimagining the beloved 1990s knit. Many had expected a faithful reinterpretation of the original, only to find that the new garments were composed of 80 percent polyester and 20 percent elastane. The synthetic material make up quickly led to a wave of criticism on social media.
That reaction reflects a broader shift in how consumers are evaluating their clothing. Many are turning away from synthetics, demonising them as little more than cheap plastic, and seeking out products made of natural fibres such as cotton, linen and wool, which are often portrayed as more comfortable and durable.
BoF’s Shayeza Walid (@shayeza_) explores the consumer shift putting fabric composition under the spotlight. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @shopdoen, @pact, @gap, @reformation

posts.caption:
This Sunday, @afaseleccion and @sefutbol will face off in the World Cup Final, and one team will take home the coveted golden trophy along with a custom crafted @louisvuitton trunk that safely encases the winning prize. Some New Yorkers were able to get a closer glimpse at the trophy today at the French maison’s Fifth Avenue flagship store before it heads to the pitch this weekend.
Created in the storied workshops of Asnières-sur-Seine, near Paris, the luxe trunk is covered by the signature Louis Vuitton Monogram canvas, showcasing two front panels adorned with a golden “V” – for “Victory” and “Vuitton” – painted to pay homage to the trophy’s colors.
📸: BFA, courtesy Louis Vuitton
🎥: CNN
posts.alt_text:
Shorts are creeping up. Thigh tattoos are peeking out. Mesh shirts abound. Dare we say it might be straight guy summer? In this weekend's edition of Big Style, Senior Features Reporter T.M. Brown explores how straight men are embracing their sartorial sensuality.

Our weekly Big Style newsletter excavates stylish amusements and contextualizes the world of fashion. Sign up at the link in our bio or comment "Big Style" and we'll link you up.

posts.caption:
How do you define glamour? Like the ever-elusive concept of personal style, glamour is often intangible, innate, even aura-based.
Enter 25-year-old Norwegian soccer player and Manchester City star @erling. Days after being knocked out of the World Cup quarter-finals by England, Haaland and his partner Isabel Haugseng Johansen dusted themselves off and headed to the Mediterranean for the @dolcegabbana Alta Sartoria show in Sicily on Tuesday.
Haaland, who has already caught the world's attention with his sprawling collection of Hermès bags and relatable love for a tacky trinket, was dressed to the nines.
He clashed his cream satin pants with a white double-breasted blazer, dark sunshades, a gold frog-shaped brooch and navy woven leather loafers. Most notable was his thin satin-like scarf whipped eccentrically over one shoulder, like a Milanese gentleman oozing sprezzatura.
Not since David Beckham have we seen such pizzazz and flare in soccer fashion.
Read more at the link in our bio.
📸: Antonio De Masi/Courtesy Dolce & Gabbana

posts.caption:
Sandra, a 43-year-old media lawyer based in Los Angeles, had been toying with the idea of breast augmentation since her 20s, but was always put off by the pain of the procedure, the long recovery time and the idea of having "this kind of foreign sac in your body."
So when Sandra, who wanted to protect her identity for privacy reasons, heard about a new injectable body contouring product last summer, she was intrigued. No synthetic fillers, no implanted sacs, no downtime.
"You hit a certain age and you decide, I'm going to do something for myself at this point," she said. The product she found — alloClae, pronounced allo-clay like the earthy sculpting material — is quick to administer. Nicknamed the lunchtime boob-job, it often takes less than an hour to inject and doesn't need general anaesthesia or a hospital setting. Plus, the aftercare instructions, Sandra said, were "shockingly easy." The fact that alloClae uses fat harvested from donated dead bodies, or cadavers as they're referred to in the scientific community, didn't really pose an issue. "I was fine with that," she said.
A growing number of people in the US appear to feel the same. Tiger Aesthetics, the company that manufactures alloClae, said more than 2,000 patients have been injected with it since May 2025. It's a buzzy topic on Reddit's popular plastic surgery page, as potential patients ask questions, seek reviews and swap doctor recommendations.
But for every plastic surgery clinic calling the injectable a "next-generation" advancement, there's an equal number of headlines branding it "zombie filler" or "corpse injections."
If there is a boundary line in the pursuit of the perfect look, are we finally butting up against it? What does it mean if society widely embraces injecting cadaver fat in the service of beauty? Could alloClae become as common as Botox?
Tap the link in bio to read more.
📸 Photo illustrations by Connie Chen/CNN/berkpixels/fStop/Getty Images

There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

posts.caption:
In an industry driven by seasonal trends, FELAÇI is building something slower.
Founded in London by two Pakistani siblings, the house looks beyond conventional ideas of European luxury, tracing its design language back to Pakistan’s centuries-old traditions of leather craftsmanship and textile making. Rather than treating heritage as decoration, FELAÇI transforms it into contemporary objects designed to be lived with for decades.
At the centre of that vision is Seal 22, a sharply structured black box-leather bag that blurs the line between handbag, jewellery and future heirloom. Its defining feature is a sculptural 22kt gold-plated seal, inspired by the ritual of sealing letters to protect identity and preserve what matters.
Deliberately irregular like melted wax, the hardware feels less like a logo and more like a personal emblem, giving the bag an almost ceremonial presence.
That philosophy runs through the entire house. Every detail, from the envelope construction to the jewellery-inspired hardware, is designed with permanence in mind. FELAÇI releases its pieces exclusively through made-to-order production, allowing each object to be carefully crafted rather than mass produced. The goal isn’t simply to create luxury accessories, but objects that gather character through time and are eventually passed from one generation to the next.
In a market where luxury often competes for attention through oversized logos, FELAÇI takes the opposite approach. Seal 22 speaks in quiet symbols, exceptional materials and thoughtful craftsmanship, proving that sometimes the most powerful statement is the one that doesn’t need to shout.
@felaci_official
posts.alt_text:
There has always been a fine line between glamour and excess. For Vogue Italia’s „The Celebration“ Issue, Mert Alas & Marcus Piggott erased it completely.

Released in December 2017, the editorial transformed fashion into an extravagant after-hours fantasy. Champagne overflows, sequins catch every flash, velvet, feathers and crystals collide, and every frame feels like the morning after the most exclusive party you were never invited to. More than a celebration of luxury, it became a celebration of fashion’s ability to create fantasy.

Photographed with Mert & Marcus’ unmistakable high-gloss aesthetic, every image feels cinematic, seductive and unapologetically glamorous. Patti Wilson’s styling pushes that vision even further, embracing maximalism with fearless confidence.

Nearly a decade later, The Celebration Issue remains one of the defining editorials of the late 2010s, a reminder that fashion is at its most memorable when it dares to embrace excess.

Credits

Publication: Vogue Italia, December 2017
Photography: Mert Alas & Marcus Piggott
Styling: Patti Wilson
Creative Direction: Giovanni Bianco
Editor-in-Chief: Emanuele Farneti
Hair: Odile Gilbert
Make-up: Lisa Eldridge
Manicure: Anatole Rainey
Set Design: Emma Roach
Casting: Piergiorgio Del Moro & Samuel Ellis Scheinman
Models: Anja Rubik, Anna Ewers, Aube Jolicoeur, Bella Hadid, Binx Walton, Caroline de Maigret, Catherine McNeil, Irina Shayk, Issa Lish, Jamie Bochert, Joan Smalls, Kennah Lau, Lea T, Lily Aldridge, Luka Isaac, Mariacarla Boscono, Natasha Poly, Noah Luis Brown, Sara Sampaio, Taylor Hill

posts.caption:
Drawing from Impressionism, Belle Époque elegance and Galliano’s signature theatrical storytelling, the collection transformed couture into moving canvases. Hand-painted florals, delicate embroidery, layered tulle and flowing silhouettes gave each look the appearance of a living masterpiece, while every garment showcased the extraordinary craftsmanship of the Dior ateliers.
More than its beauty, however, it was the collection’s influence that secured its place in fashion history. At a time when minimalism still dominated much of the industry, Galliano proved that emotion, fantasy and excess could once again define luxury. His painterly approach to couture helped shape the visual language of fashion throughout the late 1990s and 2000s, inspiring a generation of designers to embrace narrative, craftsmanship and historical references rather than treating clothing as purely functional objects.
Looking back, many of today’s couture collections, filled with floral appliqués, sculptural embroidery and museum-worthy silhouettes—still echo ideas Galliano explored in Spring/Summer 1998. It remains one of the defining collections of his Dior era and a reminder that the greatest couture doesn’t simply imitate art. Sometimes, it becomes art itself.
House: Christian Dior
Collection: Spring/Summer 1998 Haute Couture
Creative Director: John Galliano
Founder: Christian Dior

posts.caption:
Long before beauty was measured by white smiles, Japan celebrated the exact opposite.
Known as Ohaguro, the centuries-old tradition of blackening the teeth became one of Japan’s most distinctive beauty ideals. Practiced for hundreds of years, especially during the Edo period—it was associated with elegance, maturity, loyalty and social status. Married women, members of the imperial court and samurai families often wore blackened teeth as a visible symbol of adulthood and refinement.
Far from being seen as unusual, Ohaguro represented beauty through cultural meaning rather than universal standards. The black lacquer-like finish contrasted with the white face powder worn at the time, creating an aesthetic that embodied sophistication and discipline. Even more remarkably, the iron-based dye used to blacken the teeth also helped protect them from decay, making the tradition both symbolic and practical.
Today, Ohaguro survives mainly in traditional theatre, historical ceremonies and cultural reenactments. Yet its legacy remains a powerful reminder that beauty has never been universal. What one generation sees as unconventional, another may celebrate as the highest expression of elegance, proving that beauty has always been shaped by culture, not by a single global ideal.

posts.caption:
The visual identity of Pirates of the Caribbean was created by British costume designer Penny Rose, who designed every film in the franchise. Drawing inspiration from maritime history, museum collections and pirate historian David Cordingly, she blended historical authenticity with fantasy to create a world that felt believable rather than historically exact.
Every costume reflected its character. Captain Jack Sparrow’s layered scarves, weathered fabrics, rings and countless trinkets told the story of a pirate who had spent years collecting pieces of his life at sea. Elizabeth Swann’s wardrobe evolved from elegant aristocratic gowns into practical pirate clothing, while Captain Barbossa’s rich brocades and dramatic coats reinforced his commanding presence.
What truly set the costumes apart was their craftsmanship. Every garment was hand-aged, dyed, distressed and repaired to look genuinely lived in. Salt stains, faded fabrics and worn leather ensured nothing appeared freshly made, making the entire world feel authentic.
More than two decades later, the wardrobe remains one of cinema’s most influential achievements. Penny Rose didn’t simply design costumes—she created a visual language that made Pirates of the Caribbean instantly recognizable and continues to inspire fashion, film and costume design today.
Costume Designer: Penny Rose
Director (The Curse of the Black Pearl): Gore Verbinski
Producer: Jerry Bruckheimer
Historical Research: David Cordingly
Films: Pirates of the Caribbean franchise (2003–2017)

posts.caption:
Debbie Harry didn’t just become the face of punk, she also redefined what punk could look like.
At a time when the movement was dominated by aggression and anti-fashion ideals, Harry introduced something unexpected: glamour. She mixed thrift-store finds with designer pieces, leather jackets with sequins, bleach-blonde hair with smeared eyeliner, proving that rebellion and elegance could exist in the same outfit. Her style never felt calculated. It felt instinctive.
As the frontwoman of Blondie, Debbie Harry became one of the first musicians whose wardrobe was as influential as her music. She moved effortlessly between New York’s underground clubs, the art scene surrounding Andy Warhol, and the emerging world of high fashion, inspiring designers including Stephen Sprouse, Jean Paul Gaultier, Anna Sui and countless others. Decades later, her fingerprints can still be found in collections by houses like Saint Laurent, Gucci and Balenciaga.
What made Harry so influential wasn’t that she followed trends, it was that she ignored them. She wore vintage before vintage became fashionable, mixed luxury with everyday clothing long before it became standard, and treated personal style as an extension of attitude rather than status.
Long before “model off-duty” or “downtown cool” became fashion clichés, Debbie Harry had already perfected them. She didn’t just influence punk style, she permanently changed the way fashion understands cool.

“For me, ‘slut’ is not a specific type of person, sexuality, aesthetic, or nightlife identity. It is a mirror,” he says. “The queer community has deeply inspired me and #DAMUR through its courage to let go of expectations and live with freedom, honesty, and a self-defined identity.” - Damur Huang⁠
⁠
A skatepark, a runway, and a 10-hour rave. For SS27, @damur.fashion transformed Berlin's Skatehalle into an immersive collision of fashion, dance and club culture, culminating in Techno Sluts, an all-night celebration of movement, freedom and queer expression.⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to see more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @elliothoste⁠
📸 Kristina Subotina⁠
⁠
#DazedFashion

posts.caption:
Fresh off appearing in @madonna’s Confessions II short film last month, Venezuelan avant-pop visionary @arca1000000 has just announced her ninth album, XXXXX. ⁠
⁠
Known for futuristic, deconstructed cub sonics, and rich collaborations spanning everyone from Björk to Dean Blunt, the new project seems set to continue Arca’s ambitious world-building. The newly-unveiled album cover features the prolific artist sitting inside a strange, spaceship-like machine accompanied by two owls — reportedly chosen for being symbols of protection and the underworld in Greek mythology. ⁠
⁠
“It’s playful but also mischievous, to interrupt [people’s] autopilot,” says Arca of the project.⁠
⁠
XXXXX releases Friday 31st July, and will be teased in a @danielsannwald -directed livestream this Thursday 23rd.⁠
⁠
📸 @saint________________________⁠
⁠
#Dazed #Arca

posts.caption:
Spain are world champions. 🇪🇸 After a tense 1–0 victory over Argentina, the final whistle brought an unforgettable World Cup to a close. Eighteen years after Lionel Messi and a one-year-old Lamine Yamal appeared together in a UNICEF campaign, the pair met on football's biggest stage in what may be Messi's final World Cup.⁠
⁠
From unforgettable upsets and emotional fan moments to political controversy, FIFA criticism and a half-time show that had everyone talking, this tournament delivered drama at every turn. We look back at the biggest, boldest and most heartwarming moments from the 2026 World Cup ➡️⁠
⁠
📸 Via @gettyimages⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Every family has their own lore. Even the dynamics in loving families can be fraught with complex tensions and laden with inherited resentments, only detectible to those on the inside. Photographer Ashley Markle (@filmmarkle) has long used her camera as a way of laying bare and examining some of the complex history of her family and romantic relationships.⁠
⁠
Her new exhibition, Low Hanging Fruit, brings together four bodies of work, each focusing on a different significant relationship: an ex, her mum and stepfather, her husband, and her father. ⁠
⁠
Tap the link in bio to see more 🔗⁠
⁠
✍️ @ohnoitsemilyd⁠
📸 @filmmarkle⁠

posts.alt_text:
When gemstones become art.✨

Who says precious gemstones belong only in jewellery?
Rubies des Grenouilles (The Frogs’ Rubies) by Carvin French Jewelers transforms 664 carats of polished rubies into a luminous boudoir lamp, combining rubies with rock crystal, fluorite, emeralds, sterling silver and 18k yellow gold.

It’s a remarkable reminder that the world’s finest gemstones can inspire creations far beyond necklaces and rings.

Image Credits - @jewellery_masterpiece 

#DiamondWorld #BeyondJewellery #CarvinFrench #HauteJoaillerie #Luxury

posts.caption:
What if the biggest opportunity in sapphires isn’t finding a better blue… but introducing customers to colours they’ve never considered?
For generations, blue sapphire has defined the category.
Yet the sapphire family is remarkably diverse, and luxury maisons have long explored that spectrum through exceptional design and storytelling.
As coloured gemstones continue to gain attention in bridal and fashion jewellery, retailers have an opportunity to broaden how they present sapphires—not by replacing blue, but by expanding the narrative around it.
💾 Save this for your next coloured gemstone buying meeting.
📤 Share it with someone building their sapphire collection.
#GemSphere #Sapphire #ColoredGemstones #HighJewellery #JewelleryRetail

Summer is coming to an end, and a group of California prep-school seniors are about to have a very bad year. #FXPartner

FX has released a new trailer for ‘The Shards,’ Executive Producer Ryan Murphy’s adaptation of Bret Easton Ellis’s cult novel—a sun-drenched story of privilege, paranoia, obsession, and creeping dread set against the backdrop of 1981 Los Angeles.

The early ’80s fashion alone has our attention, but with a cast that includes Igby Rigney, Kaia Gerber, Homer Gere, Hayes Warner and Graham Campbell, we’re seated.

The Shards premieres August 5 on FX and Hulu.

#theshards #breteastonellis #ryanmurphy #tv

posts.caption:
Taking a military helicopter to a third grader’s golf lesson? It’s giving little prince behavior.
A new MS Now report claims Secret Service agents are “fed up” with JD Vance’s excessive personal use of government resources and tensions are especially high after he requested a military helicopter fly his nine-year-old son to a golf lesson last week. The trip was ultimately scrapped, not because anyone objected to it, but because of bad weather…and apparently this wasn’t a one-off.
The Vances have reportedly used military helicopters for multiple personal trips, including house hunting for their growing family, with Usha expecting their fourth child this month. Defense Department budget estimates put helicopter operating costs up to $24,600 per hour and naturally, taxpayers are picking up the tab.
Anonymous Secret Service sources told MS Now the VPs’ requests are not only unprecedented but frequently come at the last minute, throwing agents’ schedules into chaos and forcing rushed security planning. “They change everything,” one source said. “They don’t stick to their schedules, and that costs shit-tons of taxpayer money.”
According to the report, agents have become so frustrated with the situation that they’ve even made unofficial merch taking jabs at Vance using his Secret Service code name, “Bobcat.” Coins and stickers with phrases like “Bobcat OTR Survivors Club” and “Advance. OTR. Repeat.,” are allegedly circulating as an inside joke.
In a statement to MS Now, the vice president’s office said, “The Vances are grateful to the men and women of the U.S. Secret Service who serve our country with distinction.”
“While protecting a Vice President with a large policy portfolio and a young and growing family presents a unique challenge, agents of the Secret Service do so with excellence every day.”
#news #secretservice #threads #reels #dc

posts.caption:
A parasitic outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a gastrointestinal illness, is reportedly affecting thousands of Americans across 31 states, causing diarrhea, nausea, dehydration, and, in some cases, hospitalizations. So far, no deaths have been reported.
Michigan has been hit especially hard, with more than 2,800 reported cases. Health officials say lettuce and other salad greens are a potential culprit, and federal and state investigators are looking into whether Taco Bell is a source of the outbreak.
This comes a year after the Trump administration gutted funding for state and local health departments and scaled back a CDC program that tracks foodborne illnesses 🙃
#dump #salad #food #news #threads

posts.caption:
Last week, Louis Vuitton won a lawsuit against Molly Tea, a China-based bubble tea brand, for trademark infringement after the tea brand’s floral logo was found to be too similar to a mark used in Louis Vuitton’s signature monogram print. The tea company was ordered to pay the French fashion giant the equivalent of $1.5m within 10 days of the ruling. However, the backlash against the ruling has afforded Molly Tea with a huge wave of support, drawing customers to storefronts in a show of solidarity, and sparking fierce criticism of LVMH online,
LVMH’s grievance with Molly Tea had to do with their use of a logo that looks similar to Louis Vuitton’s four-petal flower which was developed in the 1890s. While the logo might resemble Louis Vuitton’s signature pattern, netizens are pointing out the design’s resemblance to Chinese ancestral artworks and, such as the bao xiang hua motif, and Buddhist symbols which pre-date Louis Vuitton by thousands of years. One RedNote user described the court’s ruling as “commercial monopolization of a cultural element.”
Nevertheless, Louis Vuitton has a litigious reputation in China, having filed nearly 60 trademark infringement cases in the country this year, according to the South China Morning Post, including a recent lawsuit against China’s trademark regulator.
Molly Tea reportedly attempted to acquire trademarks for their four-petal flower logo 17 times since 2022 but faced rejections due to LV’s trademark ownership of the design. Citing these rejected applications, the Suzhou court handling Louis Vuitton’s lawsuit said that Molly Tea had “significant malicious intent” in continuing to use the logo.
LVMH’s battle with Molly Tea highlights Europe’s blind spots as it relates to Asian markets; What a brand may see as a cut-and-dry legal issue, China might see as a larger issue of cultural appropriation. Do historical designs like the four-petal flower belong to tradition or to trademark owners? Should brands even have the power to trademark ubiquitous ancient symbols in the first place?

posts.caption:
Has Haute Couture Fashion Week ever been so copacetic as Fall ‘26? It seems that the designers have reached a point of consensus, so that each show was in harmony with the next.
Of course, The Jonatthieu Anderblazy of it all is hard to ignore. Though the similarities between Dior and Chanel this week became a point of conversation, each was elegant and whimsical, and offered up titillating nods to their house’s heritages; Chanel’s ultra blown-up tweed in look 47 and Dior’s ‘Arizona Coat’ in look 29, for example.
Other little (sometimes very little) details showed up numerous times through the week. Anyone notice the little tiny flower trend? In contrast to the bulging, decadent flowers we are accustomed to seeing on runways, this week’s florals were wild, pastoral, and lively. In the case of Schiaparelli, who opened the week, they were actually alive. One look used over ten thousand flowers and required almost three thousand hours of work.
Designers also leaned into  molded bodices in a myriad of eye-catching ways. Schiaparelli molded a bodice in glazed silicone which Daniel Roseberry described as an “augmented version of reality.” The effect was so powerful that the dress flew straight off the runway and onto Zendaya, who was in London for the world premiere of ‘The Odyssey.’ Ashi Studio showed a handful of bodices, many of which seemed, frankly, like fodder for 2026 cinema press tours. And Duran Latink, in his debut couture collection for Jean Paul Gaultier, used 3D printing to create a hyperrealistic distorted male torso with hand painted birthmarks and latex nipples.
Congratulations are in order for Pierpaolo Piccioli who showed his first couture collection for Balenciaga. By focusing on the dramatic silhouette of the house and eschewing stunts, he delivered that familiar airy refinement we came to expect from him at Valentino.
In all, this HCFW embraced innovation and history at the same time; Designers practiced using textures like silicon and plastic, they continued to utilize feathers in place of furs, and they pleated their little hearts out to create magnificent displays of craftsmanship. The future of haute couture seems bright.

posts.alt_text: Where my runway judges at? ☕️✍🏽

posts.caption:
Pardon my French, but in couture’s endless game of who did it first?, let’s talk about who actually did it first. That would be Ritu Beri.
A protégée of embroidery maestro François Lesage, she became the first Indian designer invited as a guest designer on the Haute Couture calendar. She is, and always will be, THAT girl. Very First Lady of the Universe-coded.
On 17 July 1999, she presented her debut couture collection (Fall 1999/2000) at the Indian Embassy in Paris, a venue that would go on to become the backdrop for several of her shows. Behind the runway sat the now-famous Padmapani mural from the Ajanta caves. Twenty-seven years later, the same mural has the girls (grown men) fighting. Foreshadowing? Perhaps. Mrs. Beri has always possessed a curious kind of clairvoyance, the ability to spot the future of Indian fashion and arrive there before anyone else even knew the destination.
The funds were scarce. Even the models had no shoes, only feet dyed in blood-red alta. But this was an era when fashion fairy tales still had a little magic left in them. Hope and audacity could still outrun reality. Unknown designers could still assemble guest lists that included Suzy Menkes, who would go on to rave about Beri in the International Herald Tribune. Those bare red feet became symbols of sensuality. The saris and dresses were hypnotic. The audience was captivated.
Until then, the idea of India in fashion had largely been interpreted through European designers and legacy maisons. This was one of the first moments an Indian designer seized control of the lens through which the Indian story would be told. So now, while everyone wants to be the blueprint, we’re forgetting who first paved the road from Delhi to Paris. Whose ateliers cracked open the couture calendar and made space for the generations of Indian designers who followed.
In an exclusive tête-à-tête with DS, Miss ma’am Beri reflects on the realities of being first, the costs, the courage, and the satisfaction of making history.
Images: Ritu Beri, Spring 2000 Haute Couture.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Twelve years into her career, success has made Kriti Sanon (@kritisanon) more fearless, but not careless. “I have to instinctively feel that this is something I can’t let go of,” she says. The old fear of needing to constantly be on set has faded, replaced by a simple shift: “It is more about quality versus quantity now.”
For our Luxury Issue, that idea feels especially relevant. Luxury, at its most meaningful, isn’t only about access, but discernment: the ability to choose fewer, better things, and to pause without mistaking stillness for disappearance. Sanon doesn’t speak of this phase as though she’s arrived. “This is probably the most exciting phase of my life and my career so far,” she says, with gratitude in her voice — and relief, too.
Click the 🔗 in bio to read the complete interview.
On @kritisanon: Silk long-sleeve shirt, light wool single-breasted jacket, Blueprint silk twill culottes, Lauren 1980 clutch, all by @newbottega. Razzmatazz earrings, @forestofchintz. Ring by @litmusindia.
Editorial Director & Words by: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Anaita Shroff Adajania (@anaitashroffadajania)
Asst. Art Director: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar) (Cover Design)
Makeup: Adrian Jacobs (@adrianjacobsofficial)
Hair: Aasif Ahmed (@aasifahmedofficial )
Nail: Bristle Dust (@bristle.dust)
Bookings Editor: Rishith Shetty (@rishithvshetty)
Brand Solutions Manager: Rhea Sanil (@rheasanil_)
Set Designer: Bindiya Chhabria (@bindiya01)
Production: Cut Loose Productions (@cutlooseproductions)
Assisted by: Nirja Shah (@nirjashahh) , Disha Talwar (@dishatalwar_)(Bookings)
Artist Reputation Management: Spice PR (@spicesocial)
#ELLECovers #KritiSanon
posts.alt_text:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStars: Pride is not a June-only conversation. It is shifting beyond visibility and asking what comes next. What does it mean to build communities that outlast individuals? To create institutions instead of exceptions? To leave behind spaces where the next person does not have to start from scratch?

For ELLE’s Pride portfolio, designer James Ferreira (@jamesferreiralabel), drag king and theatre-maker Siaan (@mxsiaan), athlete Dutee Chand (@duteechand) and transmedia producer Zena Sagar (@zena.sagar)  reflect on the futures they are trying to build. 

Their methods differ, but their ambition is remarkably similar: to leave behind a world more expansive than the one they inherited. Visibility may open the door, but it is community, institutions and shared power that keep it open.

Bringing these stories together is illustrator Vikalp Durga Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra), whose distinct visual language expands the portfolio into a shared queer world of colour, intimacy and imagination.
.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
.
Editorial Director: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi) 
Illustration: Vikalp Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra)
Words by : Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
Cover Design: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar)
Editorial Co-ordinator: Sneh Lad (@sneh_lad) 
.
[Pride, Queer Rights]

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Victoria Harbour has been the face of Hong Kong for generations, where the city's soaring skyline meets the water in a view recognised around the world. It sets the stage for our latest cover shoot, with Jacqueline Fernandez (@jacquelienefernandez) bringing her unmistakable presence to one of the city's most iconic locations. As the skyline shifts with the light, she reminds us that the most captivating moments don't always ask for attention. Sometimes, they’re simply felt. Read our interview with the cover star at the link in bio.

#ELLECoverStar: Twelve years into her career, success has made Kriti Sanon (@kritisanon) more fearless, but not careless. “I have to instinctively feel that this is something I can’t let go of,” she says. The old fear of needing to constantly be on set has faded, replaced by a simple shift: “It is more about quality versus quantity now.” 

For our Luxury Issue, that idea feels especially relevant. Luxury, at its most meaningful, isn’t only about access, but discernment: the ability to choose fewer, better things, and to pause without mistaking stillness for disappearance. Sanon doesn’t speak of this phase as though she’s arrived. “This is probably the most exciting phase of my life and my career so far,” she says, with gratitude in her voice — and relief, too.

posts.caption:
#ELLEFashion: Charlize Theron’a looks for the Odyssey press tour definitely deserves much attention. Playing the enchanting the immortal sea nymph Calypso, Theron has skipped literal references to the character. Instead, she has built a wardrobe around sculptural silhouettes, rich textures, sharp tailoring and couture craftsmanship. Every outfit has stayed true to her personal style while giving a subtle nod to the world of the film. Styled by Leslie Fremar, her press tour wardrobe proves that great fashion does not always need theatrical styling to leave an impression. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read more
Words by: @anamm__24
#CharlizeTheron #TheOdyssey

posts.caption:
Welcome to the next era of @karolg.
Following her historic Coachella performance in April—where she became the first Latina to headline the annual music festival—the Grammy-winning Colombian pop star has been rehearsing endlessly for her biggest international stadium tour yet: Viajando por el Mundo Tropitour. Following her previous Mañana Será Bonito tour—the fifth-highest-grossing tour by a Latin artist of all time—her new show will be both a celebration of the Latin community and an expansion of her sound and performance. “People try to pigeonhole me, but I’m so many things,” she tells ELLE. “I love that my image and what I represent are deeply Latin, yet I also want to be a global artist.”
In ELLE’s Summer 2026 digital cover story, the global superstar opens up about her pride in representing the Latin community, finding herself through love and loss, and teases her next musical era: “The next version of me will be new to people, but it’s always been there.”
Read the full interview at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Elliot and Erick Jiménez @elliotanderick
Stylist: Nycole Sariol @nycolesariol
Writer: Suzy Exposito @brujacore
Hair: Dindi Hojah at Forward Artists @dindihojah
Makeup: Bo for Dior Beauty @bo_champagne
Production: Nathalie Akiya and Raz Segal at GTS Productions @gtsprod
posts.alt_text:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.

Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”

For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw

posts.caption:
If you've seen ‘The Odyssey,’ then you'll know that the term and concept of “Zeus's Law” is an integral part of Christopher Nolan's new adaptation. It functions as a version of the Golden Rule; it's also an entirely new invention in a film already unafraid of displaying creative license elsewhere. The term doesn't seem to appear in any adaptations, and seems at least slightly distinct from the Ancient Greek concept of hospitality known as “xenia.”
Still, Nolan's deployment of “Zeus's Law” is intriguing, and begs the question how it might differ from “xenia”—much less the ethics of reciprocity present in many religions and traditions. To that end, ELLE reached out to two classics professors, who explain how Nolan's approach stands up to historical reality. Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Even in the lazy dog days of summer, being a Swiftie (especially this year) means being on constant high alert.
Case in point: @taylorswift sounded the alarm for her fans this morning as they noticed that three of the singer's tracks on Spotify had their backgrounds changed. “I Knew It, I Knew You,” “I Knew It, I Knew You (Piano Version),” and the original version of “Blank Space” all got minor tweaks on the streaming platform—and subsequently sent fans overtime into theorizing mode. This all comes just one month after the newly-wed pop star was photographed heading to Electric Lady Studios, not so subtly confirming she was working on new music. Read the full investigation at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
One moment, he was breaking through. The next, he was everywhere. Our July cover star, AP Dhillon (@apdhillon), has become one of the defining voices of a generation. Through the rise, the noise and the spotlight, he’s remained focused on what matters most: the music. In our latest cover story, he opens up about balancing the artist the world knows with the person he’s always been.
Chairperson: Avarna Jain (@avarna_jain)
CEO: Debashish Ghosh
Editor: Rahul Gangwani (@rahulgangs_)
Creative Direction and Cover Story: Komal Shetty (@komal_shetty_)
Photography: Taras Taraporvala (@taras84)
Styling: Nikita Jaisinghani (@nikitajaisinghani)
Cover Design: Azad Mohan
Bookings Editor: Varun Shah (@varunnshah)
Talent Liaison: Mélange Digital (@melangedigital.co)
Producer: Manju Balakrishnan (@manju_b)
Production house: Popcorn Productions (@popcorn_mb)
Location: Ng Teng Fong Roof Garden, National Gallery (@nationalgallerysingapore)
Destination Partner: Singapore Tourism Board (@visit_singapore)
On AP
Denim Jacket by Alexander McQueen (@alexandermcqueen) from Galeries Lafayette (@galerieslafayetteindia); Studded Denim Jeans by Valentino (@maisonvalentino); Shoes by Bottega Veneta (@newbottega); Rings by Amrapali (@amrapalijewels)
An @rpsg.lifestylemedia publication by @rpsggroup.
#EsquireIndia #APDhillon #VisitSingapore
posts.alt_text:
He may be AP (@apdhillon) to the world, but he’s also Amritpal Singh Dhillon. The man who still goes to the same barber, skips a hair and grooming team because he believes imperfection is real, and often forgets just how famous he is. Fame brought him freedom, financial stability, and love from fans that money can’t buy—but it also came at a cost. Our July cover star AP Dhillon (@apdhillon) opens up about life beyond the spotlight.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption:
“You know, there comes a time at a very crucial point in your life when you become absolutely fearless and just want to work without having anything else on your mind. The only thing I think about is whether I will love doing what I’ve just committed to. Of course, you want the outcome to be proportionate to your passion, but I also know it is not in my hands. What is in my control is to make sure that whatever I say ‘yes’ to, I’m happy to do, to enjoy it thoroughly.”

posts.caption:
Where do high street brands fall as far as availability and ethics? We mapped them, to help visualize the solution 🤓⁠
⁠
In plotting brands by how well or poorly they rate by our ratings methodology and how available they are, the results made clear something we talk and talk and talk about: smaller brands are more likely to be more sustainable, by taking on measures like adopting circularity initiatives, committing to lower impact fibres, and paying workers fairly.⁠
⁠
Look to the top left as proof, where so many “Good” rated but less-available brands (Sweden’s @asket, Australia’s @spell, France’s @veja, the US’ @reformation) land in comparison to the bottom right, where brands that rate our three lowest of “It’s a Start” through “We Avoid” land: they're all mega-corporations with global availability (adidas, Nike, MAC, H&M, Lacoste, Zara, SHEIN), brands that are profiting the most but doing the least.⁠
⁠
At the other end, smaller and lower rated brands (Malin+Goetz, Sézane, J.Crew) show that there’s always room to grow at any size while bigger and higher rated brands (Aesop, Lush, The North Face) illustrate how change *is* possible.⁠
⁠
Note the lone “Great”—our highest rating—is @nudiejeans, which has remarkable ethics and availability. That’s what brands should strive for, and is proof brands can work better for people, the planet, and animals should they commit to working transparently 💚⁠
⁠
Where would your favourite brand fall on this grid? Which should we map next? Share in the comments, then visit the link to explore brand ratings at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #Shopping #SustainableFashion #Sustainability #FashionTech
posts.alt_text:
Gap has gotten in a bit of trouble for rereleasing a collection from the 2000s. Any guesses what the drama is about? 🤔⁠
⁠
Turns out, the brand’s rerelease pivots garments from cotton to polyester—and people are calling the brand out, for using the material and for creating clothes in the style of ultra fast fashion brands like SHEIN and Temu 😶‍🌫️⁠
⁠
These critiques are correct, but there’s something else that seems to be missing: Gap is a fast fashion brand. While designer collaborations and stunts like this build buzz, they’re closer to SHEIN than one might think.⁠
⁠
Swipe to explore the reaction, how Gap rates, and alternatives to patterned clothing from “Good” and “Great” brands. What are your thoughts on this? Share in the comments, then visit the link for recommendations of brands that embrace patterns ↑⁠
⁠
📸 Zac Posen⁠
⁠
#Gap #GapStudio #Shopping #SummerStyles #Sustainability

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Where do high street brands fall as far as availability and ethics? We mapped them, to help visualize the solution 🤓⁠
⁠
In plotting brands by how well or poorly they rate by our ratings methodology and how available they are, the results made clear something we talk and talk and talk about: smaller brands are more likely to be more sustainable, by taking on measures like adopting circularity initiatives, committing to lower impact fibres, and paying workers fairly.⁠
⁠
Look to the top left as proof, where so many “Good” rated but less-available brands (Sweden’s @asket, Australia’s @spell, France’s @veja, the US’ @reformation) land in comparison to the bottom right, where brands that rate our three lowest of “It’s a Start” through “We Avoid” land: they're all mega-corporations with global availability (adidas, Nike, MAC, H&M, Lacoste, Zara, SHEIN), brands that are profiting the most but doing the least.⁠
⁠
At the other end, smaller and lower rated brands (Malin+Goetz, Sézane, J.Crew) show that there’s always room to grow at any size while bigger and higher rated brands (Aesop, Lush, The North Face) illustrate how change *is* possible.⁠
⁠
Note the lone “Great”—our highest rating—is @nudiejeans, which has remarkable ethics and availability. That’s what brands should strive for, and is proof brands can work better for people, the planet, and animals should they commit to working transparently 💚⁠
⁠
Where would your favourite brand fall on this grid? Which should we map next? Share in the comments, then visit the link to explore brand ratings at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #Shopping #SustainableFashion #Sustainability #FashionTech

posts.caption:
As @mrspress points out, unless you’re putting money in someone like Mark Zuckerberg’s pockets, your posts are likely only going so far 😖⁠
⁠
You’ve felt it, we’ve felt it, but there has been a clear shift in how social media relates to conversations like #sustainability. Whether due to algorithmic changes or the platform deeming what is “important” versus what isn’t, it’s easier than ever for posts pushing for change to get lost in the digital churn 🚽⁠
⁠
Remember this, as we continue to push forward in making retail more sustainable—and trying to help more and more people connect their purchases with their values. Do you think social platforms are rewarding shopping over sustainability? Share in the comments, the visit the link to learn more about this issue →↑⁠
⁠
#Shopping #Haul #SHEIN #Zara⁠

posts.caption:
In reviewing newly rated brands over the past three months, we’ve got some good news and bad news 😬⁠
⁠
The good news? Of the 431 brands rated, 82 (19%) of rated brands received our two highest scores of “Good” and “Great”. This is an improvement from Q1, where just 8% of the 362 brands scored our highest 💫⁠
⁠
The bad news? 122 brands rated (28%) received our lowest rating of “We Avoid”. This number is technically more than the lowest rated brands in Q1, where 113 (31%) had our lowest rating.⁠
⁠
Who are these top performing, more sustainable brands? Swipe to meet them, from bridalwear from the UK to a slow fashion goth company from the US 💚⁠
⁠
Share any brands you want to see rated, in case we’re able to get through them this quarter. Visit the link for the full story too →↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #SustainableFashion #Sustainability #OOTD #SummerStyle

posts.caption:
Wouldn’t it be nice if you could drop old clothes into your garden and grow a tree? 🌳⁠
⁠
Unfortunately for now, that idea is a fantasy as compostable clothing remains in a distant future due to myriad hurdles. But the demand is there: more and more people are turning to composting as an example of a regenerative future in the present, where composting offers a clever means to keep fashion circular ⏺️⁠
⁠
Compostable fashion may not be a reality now, but many communities are pushing for this to become a reality—and brands are taking notice, to mixed results. From Stella McCartney to Under Armor to @wearkent, initiatives are in motion—but some are more performative than others.⁠
⁠
If you’re interested in a regenerative fashion future, watch the composting space. Start by reading a new story by Fru Bekefi at the link, who explores how this technique could emerge in the coming years 🌱↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #Sustainability #SustainableFashion #Composting #Nature

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”

Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina

posts.caption:
One century. One standing ovation. One selection dilemma.
If Sunday's innings at #Lord's does turn out to be #RohitSharma's final ODI for #India, he could hardly have scripted a more fitting farewell.
His 138, the first ODI century by an Indian at Lord's, has reignited questions around his #ODI future and whether the BCCI can afford to look beyond him for the 2027 #WorldCup.

posts.caption:
It's a new chapter and an exciting new venture for cover star @hollywilloughby 🌟
Having been on our screens for 25 years, she’s taking her career into her own hands and launching a new multiplatform lifestyle series 'Holly Willoughby Together' next week.

posts.caption:
It's official! Andy Burnham is our new Prime Minister - yes, that makes him the fifth in four years.
‘I know people at home are fed up of politics - I hear you,' he said in his first address outside Downing Street. 'We’ve not been good enough. We’ll make this government a circuit breaker...Let’s make this the moment when Britain starts to believe again - let’s bring back hope.’
What does that look like for women? We've gone through his voting and mayoral policy history to get an idea...

posts.caption:
Welcome to the summer of #Madonna. Fresh from commanding the World Cup final stage, the Queen of Pop is proof that true icons never stand still. With Confessions II becoming her latest No.1, Madonna has now become the first female artist to top the album charts across five decades, while an entirely new generation is discovering (and dancing to) her greatest hits.
But beyond the music, Madonna’s greatest legacy may be her fearless approach to fashion. From fronting Versace campaigns to collecting awards in Tom Ford’s era-defining Gucci silk shirt and velvet trousers, or making an unforgettable entrance in Olivier Theyskens’s canary-yellow creation, every look has cemented her status as one of style’s greatest provocateurs.
Reinvented, revered and forever unrivalled. Bow down to the Queen.

posts.caption:
Finally, a trend us lazy girls can get behind😴
Nonchalant girls are racking up thousands of likes for wearing outfits that look like they were pulled off their floor, paired with barely styled hair and a make-up free face. Infuriatingly, they look amazing.
We’re calling it ‘slouchcore’ and the best part is, you probably have the tools in your wardrobe already. A fitted white tee, baggy shorts and standard black flip-flops, and you’re already halfway there to achieving the ultimate IDGAF look.
Want to know how to jump on the most effortless yet intentional trend of the season? Tap the link in bio to read more.

posts.caption:
Fashion has no shortage of beautiful faces, but @alexconsani represents a new generation of supermodels—the ones not just walking the runway but creating platforms, building communities, and leading with personality. First gaining attention on TikTok, Consani is now one of the world’s most sought after models. “I’m having fun,” says Consani. “I’m doing it for all 50 of the other bitches that would love to be in that room.” For Bazaar’s Summer 2026 issue, the supermodel talks about trying her hand at acting, moving into her big-girl home, and entering her “experimental era.”
-

Styling: @raeboxer

posts.alt_text: It's a new era for @zendaya. After closing out a historic press run and movie release with #TheOdyssey, the superstar is turning her focus on what's next... #SpidermanBrandNewDay. posts.caption:
Apparently, the AI premium applies to leather jackets too. Jensen Huang’s signed @tomford jacket, which retailed for just under $10,000, sold at @sothebys for $960,000 after 65 bids from 45 collectors.⁠
⁠
Worn by the @nvidia CEO during a 2023 Foxconn event, the jacket has become a physical artifact of the AI boom. All proceeds will support fellowships, grants, and research residencies at the Edge Institute.

@newbottega turned its latest fragrance line into an ice cream stand in London, serving scent-inspired flavors in cups designed to resemble the house’s signature Intrecciato weave. Each scoop came with its corresponding fragrance, making it part dessert, part perfume counter.

Style has reached a new peak thanks to climbing culture.
Dressed in pieces from @mountainhardwear’s new “Float On” collection, we asked real climbers at @metrorock_bushwick how their fashion tastes carry over into the gym, and how the gym influences how they approach stylistic self-expression.
Click the link in bio for more.
📸: @_ian_ohara
Styled by @konquestky

Your next jacket might come with bug spray built in.⁠
⁠
Brands like @hellyhansen, @llbean, and @columbia1938 are turning mosquito-proof technology into genuinely wearable clothing, weaving insect repellency into everything from clean-lined outerwear to relaxed safari shirts. With ticks and toxic caterpillars on the rise, bug-proof fashion could be the next GORE-TEX.
Our favorite finds from the new Chanel Fall-Winter collection 🖤✨ Impossible pieces que hoy están disponibles en HoS, dm us!

posts.caption:
Summer doesn’t arrive all at once. It unfolds in moods, moments, and memories. ☀️🌿
@ahsaassy_ steps into the season’s softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature’s quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.✨
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn’t about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.
